Recording State in a Push-Buffer

While recording a push-buffer, there are many instances in which more state is recorded into the push-buffer than is specified by the function. For example, the hardware combines both the D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEANTIALIAS and D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEMASK states into one register. So if the following is called while recording a push-buffer:

pDevice->SetRenderState(D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEANTIALIAS, TRUE);

both the state of D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEANTIALIAS and D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEMASK are recorded. Consequently, in this example, it is not possible to set D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEMASK to some value before executing the push-buffer and have it apply to the entire push-buffer, even though D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEMASK was never set while the push-buffer was recorded.

The net effect is that in the strictest case, push-buffers must effectively be executed with exactly the same state set at IDirect3DDevice8::RunPushBuffer time as was set when IDirect3DDevice8::BeginPushBuffer was first called to begin the recording; otherwise unpredictable behavior will result. In practice, since extra push-buffer state is only recorded in related state bundles, there is typically a large amount of state that can effectively be inherited at run time for bundles where none of the state was changed. An example of this is when only a IDirect3DDevice8::DrawIndexedPrimitive call is recorded into a push-buffer: since no state is changed in the push-buffer, the push-buffer can effectively inherit all the state from the caller.

In a related caveat, Direct3D does not track any state that is changed by a push-buffer. So the caller is responsible for ensuring that the state of the hardware is consistent with what Direct3D expects it to be after the push-buffer (or multiple push-buffers) is executed. For example, if the push-buffer sets D3DRS_MULTISAMPLEANTIALIAS during the course of its execution, either the push-buffer itself should reset it back or the caller should call IDirect3DDevice8::SetRenderState and manually reset the render state after executing the push-buffer. Failure to do this may result in invalid state being inherited when subsequent Direct3D commands are invoked (for example, Direct3D may not realize that some state needs to be reset).

See Also
